sn45.2:1.1: This incident is relayed by the Buddha to Pasenadi at SN 3.18:3.1. Placed so prominently, it emphasizes the role of friendship in the path.
sn45.2:3.2: This formula is highly characteristic of the Mahāvagga. In the rare cases it appears outside the Saṁyutta it is in passages identical to here. The four terms can be understood both as a sequential deepening of practice towards realization, and as synonyms for awakening or Nibbāna. The meaning depends on context, so here it means “seclusion” from wrong view; the “fading away”, “cessation”, and “letting go” of wrong view. | Viveka (“seclusion”) means to distinguish or differentiate (Chāndogya Upaniṣad 6.9.2). In Buddhism it can mean physical separation, but often means separation from defilements, especially through the practice of absorption. | Virāga has a dual sense in Pali: a state of “dispassion” where lust (rāga) is absent, and the process of defilements “fading away”, which leans on the sense of rāga as “dye” (eg. the “fading away of ignorance” at SN 12.51:9.4). | The mention of “cessation” (nirodha) further links the path to dependent origination (SN 12.2:15.4). | Vossagga is one of many words in Pali having the sense “letting go”. For Jains, it meant giving up the body (Uttarādhyayanasūtra 30.6), for Vedists, dismissing the deities at the completion of the sacrifice (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 1.9.2.27). In the Suttas, it is a word for generosity (SN 11.11:1.8), and for giving up defilements on entering absorption (SN 48.9:4.2).
